node_2: efficient tuning of large language models
edge: LLMs performance is further enhanced through efficient tuning of large language models.
There is a surge of interest in parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods for adapting pre-trained frozen LLMs efficiently to new tasks.
Adapters are one of the methods used in PEFT, as they insert trainable layers.
BitFit is another method used in PEFT that tunes the bias parameters.
Prompt tuning is also a method in PEFT that appends parameters to the input embedding layer.
LoRA is a method within PEFT that learns low-rank matrices within specific dense layers.
The existing PEFT methods have not proven suitable for efficient tool learning, unlike ToolkenGPT.
Utilizing existing PEFT methods on tool demonstrations may not efficiently capture the desired tool knowledge as ToolkenGPT does.
The authors are the first to explore efficient tuning methods for predicting tools using ToolkenGPT for tool learning of massive tools.

In ReAct, learning to call applicable tools becomes challenging as the number of tools increases, particularly for FuncQAone dataset. ReAct is susceptible to missing tool calls and making incorrect predictions in this scenario.

Similarly, ReAct faces challenges in calling applicable tools for the FuncQAmulti dataset. The increased number of tools makes it difficult to include demonstrations of every tool in the limited context, leading to potential errors in tool calls and argument predictions.

node_2: current fine-tuning and in-context learning paradigms
edge: The approach of ToolkenGPT overcomes the limitations of current fine-tuning and in-context learning paradigms.
ToolkenGPT introduces the concept of toolken embedding for each tool, enabling LLMs to call and use different tools as easily as generating word tokens.
Toolken embeddings significantly enhance LLM performance on tasks like numerical reasoning, knowledge-based question answering, and embodied plan generation.
ToolkenGPT is able to rapidly adapt and leverage new tools, demonstrating its capacity to keep pace with the constantly evolving landscape of massive tools.
We expect future research to learn robust toolken embeddings not only from demonstration data, but also other rich forms of experience [24], such as tool descriptions and input-output records.
